elements.sent-2411-001: The mathematical modeling of crowds is complicated by the fact that crowds possess the behavioral ability to develop and adapt moving strategies in response to the context.
elements.sent-2411-002: For example, in emergency situations, people tend to alter their walking strategy in response to fear.
elements.sent-2411-003: To be able to simulate these situations, we consider a kinetic model of crowd dynamics that features the level of stress as a parameter and propose to estimate this key parameter by solving an inverse crowd dynamics problem.
elements.sent-2411-004: This paper states the mathematical problem and presents a method for its numerical solution.
elements.sent-2411-005: We show some preliminary results based on a synthetic data set, i.e., test cases where the exact stress level is known and the crowd density data are generated numerically by solving a forward crowd dynamics problem.
elements.sent-2411-006: Stampede-related injuries and deaths continue to happen at events like concerts and religious, festive, or sport gatherings.
elements.sent-2411-007: See Fig.~\ref{fig:Torino for an example captured on security cameras.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Since experiments on panicked people are unethical and potentially harmful, the most practical and effective way to improve crowd management is via modeling and computational simulations.

The stampede led to 3 people dead and over 1600 injured. Video available at \url{https://youtu.be/yuqcNgcgzIA?feature=shared}.} \label{fig:Torino} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-009: Unlike inert matter, crowds possess the behavioral ability to develop and adapt moving strategies in response to the context, which significantly complicates their mathematical modeling.
elements.sent-2411-010: See Refs.~\citenum{Bellomo2022,Bellomo2023 for recent reviews on the topic.}
elements.sent-2411-011: A consequence of this complication is that, although the mathematical modeling of crowd motion has fascinated researchers since the 1950s, the large majority of existing mathematical models assume that pedestrians do not alter their, typically rational, walking behavior.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, in emergency situations, people may behave irrationally in response to fear.
elements.sent-2411-013: Thus, an accurate model of crowd dynamics in stressful situations cannot be fully captured by classical mechanics and must include heterogeneous behavior of individual entities and their interactions, accounting for the effects of the individuals’ levels of stress and its propagation.~\cite{PhysRevE.75.046109,Helbing2009,BELLOMO20161,WIJERMANS2016142,HAGHANI201724 While the widely used agent-based models~\cite{Helbing2000,WANG2015396,Zou2016,PhysRevE.98.032312,8667341 successfully account for heterogeneous individual behavior (each agent can develop a specific walking strategy), they fail to reproduce non-local and nonlinearly additive interactions, becoming progressively less accurate as the number of individuals increases.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Thus, in this paper we focus on an alternative model that can reproduce such complex interactions.
elements.sent-2411-015: Taking inspiration from the kinetic theory of gases, kinetic models of crowd dynamics derive a Boltzmann-type evolution equation for the statistical distribution function of the position and velocity of the pedestrians.
elements.sent-2411-016: In these models, people are seen as active particles, as opposed to classical particles in gases.
elements.sent-2411-017: This leads to a key difference: the interactions in kinetic models for gas dynamics are conservative and reversible, while the interactions in the kinetic models for crowd dynamics are irreversible, non-conservative and, in some cases, nonlocal and nonlinearly additive.
elements.sent-2411-018: Hence, kinetic models can overcome the limitations of agents-based models in terms of complexity of interactions.
elements.sent-2411-019: An important consequence of the aforementioned difference is that often for active particles the Maxwellian equilibrium does not exist.~\cite{Aristov_2019 The reader interested in introductory concepts on the kinetic theory of active particles is referred to Refs.~\citenum{Bellomo2013_new and \citenum{BellomoBellouquid2015 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: More recent developments can be found in Refs.~\citenum{Bellomo2017_book,Bellomo2015_new,Bellomo2019_new and \citenum{kim_quaini .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Refs.~\citenum{Bellomo2022,Bellomo2023 focus on aspects specific to the modeling of crowds taking into account behavioral dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-022: A recent interesting spin on kinetic models as possible mathematical models for artificial intelligence, i.e., collective behavior of artificial (instead of natural) self-organizing systems, is discussed in Ref.~\citenum{BELLOMO20241 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: The kinetic model considered in this paper was initially proposed in~\cite{Bellomo2013_new and further developed in \cite{Bellomo2015_new,Agnelli2015,kim_quaini .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: It assumes discrete walking directions, a continuous deterministic walking speed, and it accounts for the presence of walls and other kinds of obstacles in the walking domain.
elements.sent-2411-025: The kinetic models in \cite{Bellomo2013_new,Bellomo2015_new,Agnelli2015,kim_quaini features the level of stress as a parameter, that in the numerical experiment is set to be constant in space and time.}
elements.sent-2411-026: This homogeneity of the stress level in a crowd is obviously not realistic.
elements.sent-2411-027: Hence, some models have introduced the emotional state as a variable that, in response to interactions with other people, can change in space and time and, in turn, alter the walking strategy~\cite{Bellomo2019_new,Bertozzi2014ContagionSI .}
elements.sent-2411-028: In fact, it is known that emotional contagion significantly affects the overall crowd dynamics in complicated real-life situations such as emergency evacuations~\cite{HAGHANI201724 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: A multiscale (from agent-based to continuum) approach to crowd dynamics with emotional contagion is introduced in~\cite{Bertozzi2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Therein, the stress level is propagated by a Bhatnagar–Gross–Krook-like model and results are limited to one space dimension.
elements.sent-2411-032: While treating the emotional state as a variable leads to more realistic simulation results than representing it as a constant parameter~\cite{Bellomo2019_new , the computational cost of an additional variable can become significant as the domain size increases.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Since the overall goal of crowd dynamics simulations is to contribute to crowd management in emergency situations~\cite{Banda2020 , the computational cost of realistic simulations must be contained.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Thus, in this paper we propose a third alternative to treat the emotional state.
elements.sent-2411-035: The computational cost is contained by considering the emotional state, i.e., the stress level, as a parameter that is optimized in space and time to match density data extracted from video frames for realism.
elements.sent-2411-036: With a large database of videos such as the one in Fig.~\ref{fig:Torino , one could split the database into validation and training datasets and use the videos in the training set to learn the evolution of the stress level via an optimization procedure that minimizes the difference between the density data extracted from the video and the simulated crowd density.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In this paper, we present a possible optimization approach for this purpose.
elements.sent-2411-038: Parameter learning techniques have been proposed for agent-based models.~\cite{Lee2007,BERA201668,YAO2019314 However, to the best of our knowledge, it is the first time that such a technique is proposed for a kinetic model.}

elements.sent-2411-044: In this work, we assumes that such estimated crowd density, denoted by $\rho_v$, is available and we minimize the difference between $\rho_v$ and the crowd density computed by our kinetic model, which depends on the stress level parameter.
elements.sent-2411-045: This minimization problem yields an estimation of the stress level, so it can be considered an inverse problem.
elements.sent-2411-046: In fact, inverse problems can be used for parameter identification of mathematical models, also called forward problems, whose state variables are observed through available data.
elements.sent-2411-047: In our case, the forward problem is our kinetic model for crowd dynamics, the state variables are the probability distribution functions, from which crowd density can be computed, and the observed data are the crowd densities extracted from video images.
elements.sent-2411-048: In this paper, we solve the inverse problem as a constrained optimization problem, where the kinetic model is the constraint and the functional to be minimized is a measure of the mismatch between the density data and the computed density solution.
elements.sent-2411-049: In our approach to the constrained minimization problem, the kinetic model is first discretized in time and then the optimization step is performed.
elements.sent-2411-050: We present a numerical method for its solution.
elements.sent-2411-051: In this first work, we consider the simple geometrical settings used in the experiments on ants \cite{SHIWAKOTI20111433 and synthetic data, i.e., obtained by a forward crowd dynamics problem.}

elements.section-2411-002: The time continuous inverse crowd dynamics problem
elements.sent-2411-057: Let $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R ^2$ denote a bounded walkable domain.}
elements.sent-2411-058: We assume that the boundary $\partial \Omega$ includes an exit $E$, which could be the finite union of disjoint sets, and walls $W$.
elements.sent-2411-060: Let $\x=(x,y) \in \Omega$ denote position and ${\v =v (\cos \theta, \sin \theta) \in \Omega_{\v}$ denote velocity, where $v$ is the velocity modulus, $\theta$ is the velocity direction, and $\Omega_{\v \subset \mathbb{R ^2$ is the velocity domain.}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: For a system composed by a large number of people distributed inside $\Omega$, the distribution function is given by \begin{equation}f= f(t, \x, \v)\quad for all \,\,\, t \ge 0, \,\, \x \in \Omega, \,\, \v \in \Omega_{\v}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-062: Since we use polar coordinates for the velocity, we can write the distribution function as $f= f(t, \x, v, \theta)$, where $v$ is the walking speed and $\theta$ is the walking direction.
elements.sent-2411-063: Under suitable integrability conditions, $f(t, \x, v, \theta)d \x d \v$ represents the number of individuals who, at time $t$, are located in the infinitesimal rectangle $[x, x+dx] \times [y, y+dy]$ and have a velocity belonging to $[v, v + dv] \times [\theta, \theta+d\theta]$.
elements.sent-2411-064: For simplicity, we assume $\theta$ can take values in the set: \begin{equation}I_{\theta}=\left \{ \theta_{i}= \frac{i-1}{N_d} 2\pi : i = 1, \dots, N_d \right \},\end{equation} where $N_d$ is the maxim number of possible directions.
elements.sent-2411-065: The scale of observation for a kinetic model can be considered between the scale of agent-based models (i.e., microscopic) and the scale of continuum models (i.e., macroscopic).
elements.sent-2411-066: Hence, the assumption of discrete walking directions is reasonable because if there was a large enough crowd in every possible walking direction then a macroscopic model would be more suited.
elements.sent-2411-067: To further simplify the problem, we model the walking speed $v$ as a continuous deterministic variable that evolves in time and space depending on the crowd density.
elements.sent-2411-068: This is also a reasonable assumption since the walking speed of a person typically depends on on the level of congestion around them.
elements.sent-2411-069: Since $v$ is now a deterministic variable, we can reduce the distribution function to \begin{equation}\label{eq:f} f(t, \x, \theta)= \sum_{i=1}^{N_d} f^i(t, \x)\delta(\theta - \theta_i),\end{equation} where $f^i(t, \x)=f(t, \x, \theta_i)$ is related to the number of individuals that, at time $t$ and position $\x$, walk with direction $\theta_i$.
elements.sent-2411-070: In equation~\eqref{eq:f , $\delta$ denotes the Dirac delta function.}
elements.sent-2411-071: From now on, we will assume that all variables are dimensionless.
elements.sent-2411-072: See, e.g., Ref.~\citenum{kim_quaini for details on how the non-dimensionalization process is carried out.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Due to the normalization of $f$ and each $f^i$, the dimensionless local density is obtained by summing the distribution functions over the set of directions: \begin{equation}\label{eq:rho} \rho(t, \x)=\sum_{i=1}^{N_d}f^i(t, \x) .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-074: As mentioned above, the walking speed is a function of $\rho$ in \eqref{eq:rho .}
elements.sent-2411-075: For simplicity, we assume that the quality of the walkable domain is high everywhere, meaning that a person can walk at the the maximum dimensionless speed (i.e., 1) everywhere in $\Omega$.
elements.sent-2411-076: This maximum walking speed can be kept so long as the density around a person does not reach a critical value $\rho_c$, past which the person is forced to slow down.
elements.sent-2411-077: Following experimental estimates published in the literature, \cite{Schadschneider2011545 we set $\rho_c = 1/5$.}
elements.sent-2411-078: In the slowdown zone, i.e., for $\rho > \rho_c$, we assume the walking speed is a cubic polynomial in $\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-079: See, e.g., Ref.~\citenum{kim_quaini for the exact definition.}
elements.sent-2411-080: Figure~\ref{velocity reports $v$ as a function of $\rho$.}

elements.sent-2411-084: A model governing the dynamics of a crowd can be obtained by a balance of people, or active particles, in an elementary volume of the space of microscopic states, considering the net flow into such a volume due to transport and interactions with other people and the environment: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial f^i}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \left( \v [\rho] (t, \x) f^i(t, \x) \right) = I^i[f](t, \x) \label{eq:model}\end{equation} for $i= 1,2, \dots, N_d$.

elements.sent-2411-093: The transition probabilities $\mathcal{A _h^i$ and $\mathcal{B _{hk ^i$ are meant to account for the process through which a person decides the direction to take.}}}

elements.sent-2411-096: Given a candidate individual at point $\x$, we define their distance to the exit as \begin{equation}d_E(\x)= \min_{\x_E \in E} || \x-\x_E ||,\end{equation} and we consider the unit vector $\u_E(\x)$, pointing from $\x$ to the exit.. See Figure~\ref{domain .}

elements.sent-2411-198: The numerical experiments described in this section are inspired from the lab experiments on ants reported in Ref.~\citenum{SHIWAKOTI20111433 , which were aimed at understanding the effects of structural features on collective movement patterns during rapid egress.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Of the four scenarios studied in Ref.~\citenum{SHIWAKOTI20111433 , we consider the following three:.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Ants escaping from a circular chamber without partial obstruction near exit in Sec.~\ref{sec:circular .}
elements.sent-2411-201: Ants escaping from a circular chamber with partial obstruction (via a column) near exit is Sec.~\ref{sec:circular_col .}
elements.sent-2411-202: Ants escaping from a square chamber with exit at the corner of the walls in Sec.~\ref{sec:square .}
elements.sent-2411-203: The exit in each chamber is such that it allows unimpeded passage of a single ant or somewhat encumbered passage of two ants at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-204: Several repetitions of these scenarios were performed by the authors of Ref.~\citenum{SHIWAKOTI20111433 .}
elements.sent-2411-205: In each experiment, the ants (numbering 200–250) were allowed to nest naturally in the chamber.
elements.sent-2411-206: Then, citronella (an insect repellent liquid) was injected through a small hole in the chamber ceiling to create panic.
elements.sent-2411-207: Upon the injection of the liquid, ants rushed to the exit in a manner reminiscent of humans in a crowd panic.
elements.sent-2411-208: We start our simulations right after the citronella has been injected.
elements.sent-2411-209: Ref.~\citenum{SHIWAKOTI20111433 reports the mean time for the first 50 ants to escape the chamber.}
elements.sent-2411-210: The data were obtained by manual counting from playback of digital video recordings.
elements.sent-2411-211: Our simulations try to match those averages by correcting the fear level through the optimization procedure described in Sec.~\ref{sec:num_sol .}
elements.sent-2411-212: As mentioned in Sec.~\ref{sec:forward , we work with nondimensional quantities.}

elements.sent-2411-289: The large majority of existing mathematical models for crowd dynamics assume that pedestrians do not alter their, typically rational, walking behavior during the time interval of interest.
elements.sent-2411-290: Such models cannot be used to simulate emergency situations, like forced evacuations, when people may behave irrationally in response to fear.
elements.sent-2411-291: In this paper, we consider a kinetic model for crowd dynamics that features a parameter representing the stress level and we present a data-driven technique to set this parameter.
elements.sent-2411-292: The data-driven technique is a minimization approach, based on an inverse crowd dynamics problem and a suitable functional measuring the distance between given crowd density data and the numerical solution.
elements.sent-2411-293: We applied this new methodology to model problems inspired from the experiments on panicked ants in Ref.~\citenum{SHIWAKOTI20111433 , with synthetic data generated by numerical simulations of the forward problem.}
elements.sent-2411-294: Our numerical experiments indicate that regularization is needed to obtain computed results that match well the density data.
elements.sent-2411-295: We used a regularization term that requires a reference value for the stress level parameter, i.e., one needs a good guess for the stress level on order for the proposed approach to yield accurate results.
elements.sent-2411-296: The next step is to apply our methodology with the data extracted from the video recordings of the experiments in Ref.~\citenum{SHIWAKOTI20111433 and further investigate the sensitivity to the reference stress level.}
